coredata.dc:title: Highly stable N-doped carbon dots as the sensitive probe for the detection of Fe3+
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 Korean Physical Society. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Korean Physical Society. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Youjun
Yu, Lixin
Deng, Yakun
Peng, Kangliang
Huang, Shuai
coredata.dc:description: The nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) are facilely fabricated by a one-step hydrothermal strategy using citric acid and o-phenylenediamine as start ingredient. The as-synthesized NCDs with a high quantum yield of 38.63% at 370 nm excitation maintain significant fluorescence stability in various complex environments, indicating that NCDs have the possibility of application in harsh environments. Moreover, after adding the Fe3+ to this as-synthesized NCDs, the emission intensity at 446 nm is remarkably reduced and exhibits line-dependent relationship in the range of 0.5–90 μM with a detection limit of 0.37 μM. Meanwhile, the fluorescence lifetime and absorption characteristics are compared respectively, and Stern−Volmer is calculated. These results illustrate that the quenched fluorescence is attributed to the synergistic effect of internal filtration effect (IFE) and static quenching. Moreover, this carbon-based sensing material has elevated efficiency in detecting Fe3+ in practical samples, implying that the sensing probe provides a fast and accurate new technique for the detection of Fe3+.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fe3+
High quantum yield
Fluorescence
Complex environments
Internal filtration effect
Probe

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence spectra of NCDs synthesized at 180 °C for the different times (a) and reacted for 9 h under different temperatures (b) (Under the excitation of 370 nm).
TEM micrograph (a) and corresponding size distribution (b) of NCDs; XRD pattern (c) and Raman spectra (d) of NCDs.
XPS survey (a) and high-resolution spectra of C1s (b), N1s (c) and O1s (d) for NCDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption (black line) and maximum excitation fluorescence of NCDs in aqueous solution, UV–vis absorption (green line) of Fe3+. Inset: Photographs of NCDs in visible light (left) and 365 nm UV lamp (right). (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of NCDs aqueous solution excited with different wavelengths. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Fluorescence emission spectra of the NCDs under different pH.
Fluorescence emission spectra under different illumination time (a), concentration of NaCl (b), concentration of N2H4·H2O (c) and concentration of H2O2 (d).

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of solution NCDs after adding different concentrations of Fe3+ (0–90 μM) (inset: photographs of Fe3+ absent and present NCDs under UV light). (b) The linear equation of (F0–F)/F0 versus Fe3+, where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the NCDs at 446 nm in the without and existence of Fe3+, respectively.
Impact of potential disturbing ions on the detection of Fe3+.
F/F0 response of NCDs towards Cu2+ and Fe3+ upon addition of L-Cys.
(a) Fluorescence lifetime of NCDs in the absence and existence of 40 μM and 90 μM Fe3+. (b) Stern-Volmer fitting plots with varying concentrations of Fe3+ for different temperatures.
The Zeta potential of the NCDs and NCDs-Fe3+ complex.
Comparison of reported carbon dots and as-fabricated NCDs for Fe3+ detection.
NCDs in water samples for the detection of Fe3+.

head.ce:title: Highly stable N-doped carbon dots as the sensitive probe for the detection of Fe3+

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Materials, Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330031, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics and Materials, Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330031, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Physics and Material, Nanchang University, 999 Xuefu Road, Nanchang, 330031, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) are facilely fabricated by a one-step hydrothermal strategy using citric acid and o-phenylenediamine as start ingredient. The as-synthesized NCDs with a high quantum yield of 38.63% at 370 nm excitation maintain significant fluorescence stability in various complex environments, indicating that NCDs have the possibility of application in harsh environments. Moreover, after adding the Fe3+ to this as-synthesized NCDs, the emission intensity at 446 nm is remarkably reduced and exhibits line-dependent relationship in the range of 0.5–90 μM with a detection limit of 0.37 μM. Meanwhile, the fluorescence lifetime and absorption characteristics are compared respectively, and Stern−Volmer is calculated. These results illustrate that the quenched fluorescence is attributed to the synergistic effect of internal filtration effect (IFE) and static quenching. Moreover, this carbon-based sensing material has elevated efficiency in detecting Fe3+ in practical samples, implying that the sensing probe provides a fast and accurate new technique for the detection of Fe3+.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are an emerging carbon nano-material, which have been widespread studied in terms of its excellent fluorescent characteristics, low toxicity, superb photostability, environmental friendliness and ideal biocompatibility, etc [1–4]. CDs have displayed obvious advantages in medical imaging technology, drug delivery, chemical, biological sensing, bioimaging and photodynamic therapy [5–7]. Most transition metals are found in the earth's crust as oxides or sulfides, which exhibit a variety of biological effects on animals, plants, and human bodies [8]. Consequently, the monitoring transition of metals has aroused great interest. To explore the detection of transition metals, previous studies have considered the use of fluorescent nanomaterials for their selective detection [9–13].

Iron is found in many forms in the body, and its physiological functions include oxygen transport [14], enzymatic reactions [15], and hemoglobin synthesis [16]. Imbalance of iron in human cells can be contributed to serious impairment of vital systems, for example, neurodegeneration, heart disease, cancer, liver damage, and inflammation [17–20]. Thus, it is crucial to build a framework for tracking Fe3+ in living systems. At present, voltammetry, atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS), and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICPMS) [21] are traditional for analytical methods of Fe3+, which greatly limits their application due to complex instruments and cumbersome sample preparation procedures needed in the actual operation process. There is therefore an urgent need to explore a novel Fe3+ detection probe with superior sensitivity, simple and rapid operation.

In the last decade, a wide variety of approaches to synthesize CDs have been successfully developed. At present, the synthetic method of CDs can be classified into two categories. One is the bonding method of products, which involves physical synthesis by arc discharge [22,23], laser ablation [24–26], and chemical synthesis [27] by combustion and oxidation [28–31], hydrothermal, microwave, and electrochemical method. The other, different raw materials are separated into bottom-up and top-down method [32]. Hydrothermal is one of the easiest ways to prepare water-soluble CDs. The magnitude of CDs can be controlled by varying the response time and heating temperature, which has the merit of being inexpensive and environmentally friendly. The prepared CDs have abundant surface functional groups and can be easily doped with heteroatoms [33,34]. So far, the luminescence properties of CDs may be optimized by doping with sulfur, boron, phosphorus, etc., and surface modification [35–37]. To date, there is a large body of relevant literature reporting the detection of Fe3+ by CDs [13,38–40], but none of them has verified the ability to cope with the various harsh conditions under which it is applied. Therefore, finding a highly stable CDs that can respond to extreme environments has become a natural priority.

In this work, the NCDs are fabricated as fluorescent probes via a one-step hydrothermal route utilizing citric acid and o-phenylenediamine. The NCDs exhibit excellent fluorescence stability in various harsh environments. Afterwards, the fluorescence could be clearly quenched due to the formation of a non-luminescent complex between NCDs and Fe3+, demonstrating the ability of the fabricated NCDs to determine Fe3+ through the synergistic effect of IFE and static quenching. Moreover, the proposed detector is applied to the quantitation of Fe3+ in real sample to assess its analytical capability.

O-phenylenediamine (oPD), citric acid (CA), and l-Cysteine (L-Cys) are purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). FeCl3·6H2O, NaCl, BaCl2·2H2O, CaCl2, KCl, MnCl2, LaCl3·7H2O, MgCl2·6H2O, ZnCl2, CuCl2·2H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, NiCl2, FeCl2·4H2O, NH4Cl, PbCl2, HgCl2, AlCl3, CdCl2, AgNO3, CrCl3·6H2O, Na2S, Na2SO4, NaBr, NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4, Na3PO4, NaF, CH3COONa, NaNO3, NaHCO3, Na2CO3 are purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All experimental reagents are of analytical quality and can be applied directly without deeper purification.

The fluorescence spectra are measured by a Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrometer. Morphological and sizes of NCDs are recorded by the JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (TEM) (JEOL, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement is given by a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffraction machine using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.1546 nm). Raman spectra are recorded using a Horiba LabRAM HR Evolution. X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS) are determined with an ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Fisher), using monochromatic AlKα radiation (1486.6 eV). The Gaussian-Lorenzian peak shape obtained by XPS peak 41 software is used to analyze and fit the XPS after subtraction of the Shirley background. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra are carried out on the Spectrometer 10.0. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra are characterized through a Hitachi UH4150 spectrophotometer. The fluorescent lifetime of NCDs is tested by FS5 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd., UK) and Zeta potential is obtained from a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90.

Photoluminescent (PL) NCDs are produced in a one-pot hydrothermal process. In short, 108 mg of oPD and 192 mg of CA are dispersed into 20 mL deionized water and the mixture is stirred over 30 min. Then, the transparent solution is diverted to a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave maintained 180 °C for 9 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution is centrifuged (10,000 rpm, 5 min), filtrated by 0.22 μm filter membrane, and dialyzed with membrane (MWCO1000) for 24 h to remove the salt. Finally, after freeze-drying in vacuum, the powder of fluorescent NCDs is obtained for further use. Repeat the above operation, except varying the reaction conditions, including holding at 180 °C for 6 h and 12 h and setting the temperature at 160 °C and 200 °C for 9 h of reaction.

Prepare 0.04 mg/mL of NCDs solution, take 200 μL of solution in centrifuge tube each time, and add 2 mL of different concentrations of Fe3+ solution, shake well and incubate for 10 min. Then, the fluorescence intensity of the above transparent solution is calculated at 370 nm excitation wavelength under room temperature. At the same time, a similar Fe3+ detection procedure is applied to the coexistence assay of NCDs and other interfering ions.

The optimized response parameters are investigated and described in Fig. 1 . Clearly, the emission peak of NCDs is at 446 nm regardless of the modification of the reaction parameters, indicating that the NCDs have a stable emission center. According to Fig. 1a, the NCDs have the best fluorescence performance at a reaction time of 9 h. Meanwhile, Fig. 1b shows that the best reaction temperature is 180 °C. Overall, the optimal reaction conditions for NCDs are as 180 °C for 9 h. Subsequent experiments and characterization of NCDs obtained under this reaction condition are performed.

From the TEM micrograph (Fig. 2 a), the as-synthesized NCDs have a quasi-spherical shape and are well dispersed. The dimensions of 100 nanoparticles are randomly recorded in Fig. 2b, and the average diameter of NCDs is 2.95 nm, with sizes ranging from 1.28 to 5.93 nm. In order to further determine the microstructure of NCDs, the XRD pattern of NCDs is investigated. As shown in Fig. 2c, only one broad diffuse diffraction peak is observed at 23.4°, displaying an amorphous carbon phase [12]. The nanoscale size of NCDs also contributes to the broad diffraction peaks [40], which coincides with the TEM results. Besides, Raman spectra of NCDs (Fig. 2d) are measured, in which the D band (1352 cm−1) and G band (1592 cm−1) represent the disordered and graphitized structures of carbon materials, respectively [41].The ID/IG is equal to 0.76, which proves that a carbon core with some degree of graphitization has been formed.

The functional groups on the surface of NCDs are surveyed by FT-IR technology (Fig. 3 ). Two classic absorption bands emerging at 3392 cm−1 and 3070 cm−1 are ascribed to O–H and N–H, respectively. The absorption peak centered at 2931 cm−1 suggests the stretching vibration of C–H group. While the sharp absorption peak at 1911 cm−1 is induced by CC signal. Also, the absorption peak occurring at 1711-1580 cm−1 suggests the existence of CO (corresponding to the –COOH and –CONH2). Moreover, the distinctive peak at 1222 cm−1 falls within the C–N [2]. Based on the above data, nitrogen-doped CDs have been synthesized with hydroxyl, carboxy, and amine groups present on the surface of the NCDs.

To further confirm the elemental composition and functional groups, the XPS of NCDs are analyzed in Fig. 4 a. The spectra depict that the primary components of NCDs are C, N and O with the corresponding atomic percentages of 69.32%, 10.10% and 20.58%, respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (Fig. 4b) can be deconvoluted as three peaks with binding energies at 288.4 eV, 286.2 eV and 284.6 eV, which can be assigned to the CO, C–N/C–O and C–C/CC bonds, respectively [12,34]. In the N1s spectra (Fig. 4c), there exist two peaks at 400.2 eV and 398.4 eV, which are assigned to N–H and N–C [13]. O1s spectra (Fig. 4d) show two distinct peaks at 531.2 and 532.4 eV, indicating the occurrence of CO and C–OH/C–O–C [12]. These results of XPS are consistent with the analysis of FTIR, further proving that NCDs have been successfully obtained.

The UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 5 a) and fluorescence spectra of NCDs (Fig. 5b) are used to record the optical behaviors of NCDs. As seen in Fig. 5a, an explicit and well-characterized peak at 370 nm is assigned to the n-π* transition of CO bond, while the centers of absorption peaks at 269, 244, and 218 nm could be ascribed to the π-π* transitions of CC and CN bonds, respectively [42,43].

To further exploit the optical features of NCDs, its fluorescence spectra with the excitation at different wavelengths are observed in Fig. 5b. As the excitation wavelength increases from 250 nm to 410 nm, the emission at about 446 nm is observed and NCDs exhibit excitation-independence behavior, elucidating that the fluorescence mechanism of NCDs may be related to exciton band-side decay as well as surface molecular states [44,45]. It is remarkable that the fluorescence excitation curve can nicely overlap with the absorption spectra, which also proves the above viewpoint. Moreover, NCDs aqueous solution appears transparent under sunlight and exhibits intense blue light under UV irradiation (The inserted photos of Fig. 5a). Through transient-state fluorescence spectrum of as-fabricated NCDs, the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) is measured as 38.63%, implying that the NCDs possess excellent luminescent performance. To survey the stability of NCDs in aqueous solution, pH as a crucial factor affecting the fluorescence intensities of NCDs is investigated. See in Fig. 6 , under strong acidic conditions (pH < 5), the fluorescence intensity of NCDs is a slight decreased and red-shift phenomenon emerges, which may be caused by the combination of –COOH on the surface of NCDs and H+, resulting in excited proton transfer [46]. The PL of NCDs under alkaline conditions is predominantly dependent on the β-dicarbonyl group while the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups contribute relatively little [46]. The fluorescence intensity has no significant alteration with the increase of pH from 5.0 to 11.0, indicating that the amount of β-dicarbonyl on the surface of NCDs is small. The carbonyl functional group as a non-radiative trap center absorbs a large number of electrons to inhibit photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) [47]. The lack of β-dicarbonyl on the surface of NCDs might be responsible for the high QY. The normal pH of human tissue fluid is covered within 5.0–11.0, and as-fabricated NCDs have robust fluorescence properties in this region, giving them tremendous potential for applications in bioimaging and bioprobe fields.

Besides, considering that carbon-based fluorescent probes often need to cope with tough situations, illumination time, ionic strength, oxidation concentration and reduction concentration experiments are explored (Fig. 7 ). The NCDs solution is irradiated under ultraviolet lamp (365 nm) for 2 h, and then transferred to PL spectrometer (370 nm) to measure its intensity (Fig. 7a). The fluorescence emission spectra almost overlap, demonstrating the outstanding photostability of NCDs. As shown in Fig. 7b, there is a non-trivial change in the fluorescence intensity across the NaCl concentration scale from 0.25 to 1.50 mol/L, further elucidating the potential of NCDs for practical applications. Hydrazine hydrate is adopted for experiments on the resistance of NCDs to powerful reducing agents (Fig. 7c). The fluorescence intensity of NCDs exhibits subtle fluctuations in the concentration of hydrazine hydrate from 0 to 500 mM, suggesting that the as-fabricated NCDs solution have superior resistance to reduction. To check the effect of oxidation on the NCDs, different concentrations (0–500 mM) of hydrogen peroxide are added to the as-fabricated NCDs solution (Fig. 7d). The experimental concentrations are considerably larger than the ones (500 μM) previously reported [12], and the emission spectra of NCDs still remain unchanged seemingly, indicating that the antioxidant capacity of the carbon dots is satisfactory. The fact that the intensity of NCDs is virtually negligibly affected in the various harsh environments mentioned above illustrates the extremely stable nature of as-fabricated NCDs.

Given that the NCDs maintains excellent optical properties in harsh environments, we explored the possibility of using it as an Fe3+ probes. To realize the optimal sensing capability of NCDs, the fluorescence emission spectra of solutions of NCDs in the presence of different concentrations of Fe3+ are recorded. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the emission peak at 446 nm decreases progressively with increasing Fe3+ concentration under the excitation at 370 nm, and the inset reflects this change well. There is an excellent linear regression (R2 = 0.99688) between quenching ratio [(F0–F)/F0] and the different Fe3+ concentrations (0, 0.5, 0.8, 1.5, 3, 5, 30, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90 μM) (Fig. 8b). According to the signal-to-noise ratio of 3 [48], the detection limit is measured as 0.37 μM, which is far below the safe drinking water level (5.357 μM) required by the U.S. EPA [49]. Some reports of carbon dots probes for Fe3+ are listed in Table 1 [50–59]. Obviously, the NCDs prepared in this work possess prominent QY while ensuring low detection limits. In summary, as-fabricated NCDs have a sufficiently low detection limit and high sensitivity to Fe3+ in aqueous systems.

Selectivity is an indispensable factor in estimating the applicability of fluorescent probes. It is estimated the selectivity of the as-prepared NCDs for Fe3+ and a number of disturbing ions (Fig. 9 ), including the cations of Na+, Ba2+, Ca2+, K+, Mn2+, La3+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Fe2+, NH4 +, Pb2+, Hg2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Ag+, Cr3+ and Fe3+ and the anions of S2−, SO4 2−, Br−, H2PO4 −, HPO4 2−, PO4 3−, F−, CH3COOH−, HCO3 −, Cl−, NO3 − and CO3 2− at the final concentration of 100 μM. The fluorescence quenching of NCDs is almost complete when the concentration of Fe3+ reaches 100 μM, so the concentration of interference experiment is based on this. The figure shows that only Fe3+ has a significant quenching effect on the NCDs solution after the addition of various types of ions. However, after adding 100 μM Fe3+ to the coexisting solution again, a remarkable quenching phenomenon emerged. Hereby, in practical applications involving these common interfering ions, the as-prepared NCDs as in have excellent selectivity for detecting Fe3+.

When interfering ions affect the detection results, masking agents are frequently employed to eliminate the effects of disturbances [9,34]. As presented in Fig. 10 , Cu2+ has a non-negligible fluorescence quenching of the NCDs solution, which is detrimental to the reliability of Fe3+ detection. Thus, the fluorescence is recovered by adding L-Cys to the mixture of NCDs and Cu2+, implying that L-Cys exerts an excellent masking function. After further addition of Fe3+, complete quenching of the mixed solution occurred again, indicating that the NCDs regained the ability to detect Fe3+ concentration at this time.

According to previous reports, fluorescence quenching may potentially originate from FÖrster resonance energy transfer (FRET), photo-induced electron transfer (PET), IFE, dynamic quenching and static quenching [60,61]. UV–vis absorption spectra are performed firstly, and as shown in Fig. 5a, the excitation band, absorption band of NCDs and the absorption band of Fe3+ obviously overlapped around 370 nm, demonstrating that the dip in fluorescence of NCDs at 370 nm excitation is presumably due to FRET, PET and IFE. As described in Fig. 11 a, the fluorescence decay spectra with the absence and existence of Fe3+ are surveyed. No matter whether there is Fe3+ or not, the NCDs solution shows a bi-exponential curve. Simultaneously, the fluorescence lifetimes of NCDs could be calculated to be 6.77, 6.68 and 6.50 ns corresponding Fe3+ concentrations of 0, 40 and 90 μM, respectively. Remarkably, no significant change in the fluorescence lifetime is observed in the presence or absence of Fe3+, demonstrating that the sensing mechanism could well be assigned to IFE and static quenching [62,63].

To validate this assumption, the standard Stern−Volmer equation is applied [64]:
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
S V
[ Q ]
Where KSV represents the Stern−Volmer quenching constant. [Q] denotes the concentration of Fe3+. At different temperatures from 298 to 353 K, F0/F versus Fe3+ concentration exhibited a linear correlation (Fig. 11b), and the fluctuation of quenching constant (Ksv) is negligible. The average value of Ksv is 2.4 × 104 M−1 at 298, 313, 333 and 353 K, suggesting that the fluorescence quenching is due to static quenching caused by forming a stable non-fluorescent complex. To further justify the formation of non-fluorescent complex, the Zeta potential are measured (Fig. 12 ). After the addition of Fe3+, the Zeta potential value of the NCDs solution ranges from −7.85 mV to −14.45 mV, indicating that Fe3+ interacts with the amino groups on the surface of the NCDs to form a complex and hence the potential value is more negative [13,39]. Apparently, the fluorescence reduction of NCDs by Fe3+ is caused by the synergistic effect of IFE and static quenching.

To further validate the performance of the fluorescent sensor on practical samples, standard addition experiments are employed and environmental water samples are obtained from the lake of Nanchang University. The water samples are purified through centrifugation and a 0.22 μm microporous membrane before further testing. As summarized in Table 2 , the spike recoveries are between 102.1% and 123.5% with a lower relative standard deviation (RSD). The above results show that NCDs are promising and have high sensitivity in determining the concentrations of Fe3+ in real samples.

In this article, a high sensitivity and selectivity NCDs probe for rapidly detecting Fe3+ is obtained with a high QY of 38.63% using a one-step hydrothermal method. First, the conditions for the development of NCDs are optimized. Morphological and structural characterization shows that carbon cores with graphitization have been created. In addition, there are numerous functional groups on the surface of as-synthesized carbon cores, which can improve the hydrophilicity and the stability of NCDs in solution. In stability experiments, pH tests, radiation time, ion concentration, and antioxidant reducibility are measured, confirming the potential of synthetic NCDs for applications in harsh environments. Furthermore, a NCDs-based fluorescence sensor is developed for sensitive detection of Fe3+, exhibiting a significant linearity ((F0–F)/F0 = 0.0086[Fe3+] +0.02021) with a detection limit of 0.37 μM. To cope with the complicated reality, an enormous number of common interfering ions are used to observe whether they would act as quenchers for the NCDs solution. The results confirm that the NCDs solution is highly selective for Fe3+ and insensitive to other interfering ions. Meanwhile, we reveal IFE-based and static quenching sensing mechanism of NCDs for Fe3+ by absorption comparison, fluorescence lifetime and Stern−Volmer fitting plots studies. In application, the fluorescent probe is used to detect several samples from a lake, demonstrating that the probe can conveniently and rapidly detect Fe3+ concentrations.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [grant numbers No. 51762033], and Student Innovation Special Funds of Jiangxi province [grant numbers No.YC2021–B027].
